coredata.dc:title: Solvent-dependent carbon dots for multifunctional sensing of temperature, pH, and proton pump inhibitors
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Piao
Peng, Jingdong
Zhang, Zilong
Wang, Xiang
Zhu, Xiaolan
Fan, Kun
Luo, Pan
coredata.dc:description: Multifunctional sensing, a strategy to improve the efficiency of analysis and detection, has attracted much attention. In this study, a multifunction sensing platform was developed for temperature, pH, and proton pump inhibitors (PPI) with fluorescence carbon dots. Solvent-dependent carbon dots (PG-CDs) were synthesized through a one-step solvothermal method from phloroglucinol with the assistance of HCl. Base on the effect of functional groups on the surface, solvent-dependent behavior, temperature and pH responsive fluorescence can be observed. The PG-CDs display a reversible temperature-sensitive fluorescent behavior within 15–65 °C in N, N-dimethylacetamide. The fluorescence intensity of PG-CDs also responded to pH in a range of 3.0–7.0 with good reversibility and anti-interference. Therefore, the platform for temperature and pH sensing was proposed. In addition, since the synergistic effect of dynamic and static quenching, proton pump inhibitors (PPI) can be detected sensitively, including esomeprazole (EPZ), omeprazole (OPZ), and rabeprazole (RPZ). The linear range of detection for EPZ, OPZ, and RPZ is 2.0–80.0, 2.0–75.0, and 10.0–200.0 μM and the limits of detection is 0.29, 0.55, and 2.99 μM, separately. The recoveries for real samples were between 91.83 and 102.3%, which indicated that the platform for PPI sensing had good accuracy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solvent-dependent
Multifunctional sensing
Temperature
pH
Proton pump inhibitors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of light and salt concentrations

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors. The authors special thanks the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the tests of XPS and TEM.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of solvent-dependent carbon dots for multifunctional sensing of temperature, pH, and proton pump inhibitors.

(a) TEM and HRTEM images of PG-CDs; (b) the particle size distribution of PG-CDs; (c) XRD patterns of PG-CDs and phloroglucinol; (d) FT-IR spectra of phloroglucinol, O-CDs and PG-CDs; (e) the full spectrum of XPS; (f) the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s.

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectrum, excitation and emission spectra of PG-CDs in aqueous (Inset: the paragraphs under daylight ang UV light); (b) The emission spectra under different excitation (420–520 nm); (c) The normalized FL spectra of PG-CDs dissolved in different solvents; (d) The normalized FL spectra of PG-CDs dissolved in different ratios of ethanol and water solutions. (e) The absorption spectra of PG-CDs dissolved in different solvents; (f) The absorption spectra of PG-CDs dissolved in different ratios of ethanol and water solutions.

(a) The emission spectra of PG-CDs at different temperatures in DMAC; (b) The linear fitting of fluorescence intensity vs. temperature in DMAC; (c) Cycle tests under 15 and 65 °C. (d) The emission spectra of PG-CDs in BR buffer with different pH (2.0–11.0); (e) The linear relationship of FL intensity and pH; (f) UV–vis absorption spectra of PG-CDs at different pH (2.0–11.0).

(a), (c), (e) Spectra of different concentrations of EPZ, OPZ, and RPZ mixed with PG-CDs (Inset: the curve of Ln(F0/F) versus PPI concentrations; (b), (d), (f) Linear relationship of Ln(F0/F) and EPZ, OPZ, RPZ concentrations.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of EPZ, PG-CDs, the mixture of EPZ and PG-CDs, the mixture of EPZ and PG-CDs in theory; (b) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of with and without EPZ; (c) Stern-Volmer curves of 288 K and 388 K; (d) Signal response of PG-CDs to different interfering substances (the abscissas 1–29 represent blank, EPZ, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Ni3+, Al3+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cl−, I−, Br−, phenylalanine, glycine, tryptophan, lysine, l-cysteine, glutathione, glucose, sucrose, ascorbic acid, dopamine, citric acid, urea and uric acid, respectively).
Determination of EPZ in human serum.

head.ce:title: Solvent-dependent carbon dots for multifunctional sensing of temperature, pH, and proton pump inhibitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional sensing, a strategy to improve the efficiency of analysis and detection, has attracted much attention. In this study, a multifunction sensing platform was developed for temperature, pH, and proton pump inhibitors (PPI) with fluorescence carbon dots. Solvent-dependent carbon dots (PG-CDs) were synthesized through a one-step solvothermal method from phloroglucinol with the assistance of HCl. Base on the effect of functional groups on the surface, solvent-dependent behavior, temperature and pH responsive fluorescence can be observed. The PG-CDs display a reversible temperature-sensitive fluorescent behavior within 15–65 °C in N, N-dimethylacetamide. The fluorescence intensity of PG-CDs also responded to pH in a range of 3.0–7.0 with good reversibility and anti-interference. Therefore, the platform for temperature and pH sensing was proposed. In addition, since the synergistic effect of dynamic and static quenching, proton pump inhibitors (PPI) can be detected sensitively, including esomeprazole (EPZ), omeprazole (OPZ), and rabeprazole (RPZ). The linear range of detection for EPZ, OPZ, and RPZ is 2.0–80.0, 2.0–75.0, and 10.0–200.0 μM and the limits of detection is 0.29, 0.55, and 2.99 μM, separately. The recoveries for real samples were between 91.83 and 102.3%, which indicated that the platform for PPI sensing had good accuracy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a novel fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with a particle size of less than 10 nm [1], has been widely used in bioimaging, optical sensing, optoelectronic devices and catalysis owning to their favorable water solubility, high stability, low toxicity, strong photoluminescence properties, etc [2–5]. Many scientists are devoted to the research of CDs in recent years [5,6]. It is understood that most of the fluorescent carbon dots currently developed are for the determination of single target in optical sensing. It is more meaningful to realize the determination of two or more targets by one CDs, which can improve the analysis efficiency and reduce resource consumption and energy waste. Therefore, it is very important to prepare carbon dots that can be used for multifunctional sensing. We know that the choose of reaction raw materials is great important in the preparation of CDs [7,8], and it is easier to prepare target CDs by selecting suitable precursors. It is reported that many CDs have been synthesized from citric acid [9,10] or phenylenediamine [11]. In recent years, phloroglucinol (PG) with a unique symmetrical structure has gradually come into view. Inspired by the reported CDs with excellent properties prepared from phloroglucinol, such as ultra-narrow bandwidth multicolored triangular CDs [12], large-scale red luminescent CDs powders [13], high-efficiency solid-state fluorescence and dual emission CDs [14], phloroglucinol was chosen as a precursor to synthesize PG-CDs.

Proton pump inhibitors (PPI) are a class of drugs used in the treatment of gastric disorders such as hyperacidity, gastroesophageal reflux, and peptic ulcers, including omeprazole (OPZ), rabeprazole (RPZ), esomeprazole (EPZ) and so on [15]. They have been widely used in the treatment of acid-related diseases due to its safety and effectiveness. However, long-term and high-dose use of PPIs will affect the absorption of calcium, magnesium and vitamin B12 [16], causing adverse effects. The detection of PPI quantitatively is essential to avoid their overuse, and is also beneficial for the treatment of human diseases and the protection of human health. Nowadays, methods for the determination of PPI mainly contain high performance liquid chromatography [17,18], spectrophotometry [19], chemiluminescence [20], electrochemical [21], infrared spectroscopy [22]. However, these methods have the disadvantages of complex pretreatment, expensive instruments, professional operators, and low sensitivity. Therefore, it is particularly important to develop sensitive, cheap and efficient strategies for the detection of PPI. In comparison to traditional analysis methods, fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy has attracted much attention due to its merits of simple operation, low cost, fast response. To our knowledge, Fluorescence spectroscopy is rarely used to detect PPI. There is no report on the detection of PPI by carbon dots. Therefore, the use of carbon dots as fluorescent probes to detect PPI is worth studying, and it can further broaden the application of carbon dots in analysis.

Temperature and pH are critical physical and chemical parameters. The survival of cells, the growth of animals and plants and biochemical reactions are inseparable from them [23,24]. Small fluctuations in temperature and pH may cause a series of negative reactions. It is of great significance to monitor temperature pH accurately. Importantly, as for temperature sensing, semiconductor quantum dots [25], metal nanoclusters [26], rare earth element doped nanoparticles [27] and other nanomaterials are widely used, currently. However, metal elements are easy to cause environmental pollution, the material preparation process is complicated, and the size of nanoparticles is large, which hinders its development. The discovered temperature-responsive CDs avoid these problems in recent years. Although carbon dots have been used to temperature sensing, most of them are short-wavelength blue emission, which is not conducive to further application. The carbon dots with longer emission wavelength for temperature detection are still worthy of further development.

Herein, we proposed a novel carbon dots (PG-CDs) through solvothermal method from phloroglucinol anhydrous under acidic conditions provided by HCl. PG-CDs exhibit different emission in different solvents, which is solvent-dependent. It is inferred that the solvent-dependent behavior of PG-CDs is due to the effect of surface functional groups (-OH) by studying in detail. When PG-CDs are dispersed in N, N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC), the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots gradually decreases with the increase of temperature, and has a good linear relationship and reversibility, which can be used to detect temperature. At pH 3.0–7.0, with the increase of pH, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly and showed a good linear relationship, which also can be used for pH sensing. In addition, based on the synergy of dynamic and static quenching, the fluorescence intensity in the system gradually decreased in the presence of different concentrations of proton pump inhibitors, and a novel fluorescence sensing strategy for the determination of PPI was established. Based on these phenomena, a multifunctional sensor platform for temperature, pH, and PPI was developed (Fig. 1 ).

PG-CDs were synthesized by a simple solvothermal method from phloroglucinol anhydrous. For a typical procedure, 0.2 g of phloroglucinol anhydrous was dissolved in 10 mL of DMF, and 0.5 mL concentrated hydrochloric acid was added. The resulted mixture was sealed in a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 10 h. After it was cooled to room temperature naturally, the products were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. Then, adding petroleum ether and ethyl acetate (petroleum ether: ethyl acetate = 2:1) to the liquid to precipitate CDs. The powder of carbon dots was obtained by freeze-drying and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were collected at different temperatures on the FL spectrometer with a circulating water bath. PG-CDs was dispersed in DMAC, and the fluorescence spectra were collected after the temperature reached the set temperature and stabilized for 5 min at the range of 15–65 °C with an interval of 10 °C. Five cycles of heating and cooling were performed at 15 and 65 °C to explore the stability of the temperature response.

The pH sensing was carried in Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer. The prepared carbon dots were dissolved in water by sonication to get a solution with 1 mg mL−1. After mixing 100 μL PG-CDs solution with 1900 μL BR buffer with different pH, the FL spectra were measured at the excitation of 490 nm, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded at 548 nm. The pH was adjusted by 0.2 M HCl or NaOH for the reversibility tests. The anti-interference tests were conducted by adding possible interfering substances into the PG-CDs solution with pH value of 3.0.

200 μL BR buffer (pH 3.5 for EPZ, pH 4.0 for OPZ and RPZ), 100 μL PG-CDs (1 mg mL−1), 80 μL PPI solution with different concentrations, and 1620 μL distilled water were added to the 2 mL Eppendorf tube in sequence. The mixture was mixed evenly and the reaction lasted for 1 h at room temperature. The FL spectra was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 490 nm and repeated three times. The possible interfering substances (200 μM) were taken the place of PPI to investigate the selectivity.

PG-CDs were synthesized by a simple one-step solvothermal method using phloroglucinol as the carbon source and assisted by HCl. In order to explore the effect of solvents (water and DMF) and HCl on the synthesis process, the fluorescence properties of hydrothermal treatment, and HCl-free (O-CDs) were compared after reacting at the same temperature and time, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1a, there is no fluorescence can be observed in the crude product by hydrothermal treatment at the existence of HCl (Ⅰ). Using DMF as solvent, the crude product without HCl showed dusty blue FL (Ⅱ). Only the crude product with DMF as a reaction solvent and at the existence of HCl showed bright yellow fluorescence (Ⅲ). We also compared the 3D spectra of the purified PG-CDs and O-CDs. It was found that PG-CDs exhibit a single yellow emission center (Fig. S1b) while O-CDs have green emission and some blue emission (Fig. S1c). Therefore, DMF and HCl are necessary for the synthesis of single yellow emission carbon dots.

The micromorphology of PG-CDs was collected by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2 a, PG-CDs are uniformly dispersed spherical nanoparticles and obvious lattice fringes can be observed in the high-resolution TEM. The lattice spacing was calculated to be 0.22 nm, which corresponding to the (100) plane of graphene [28]. The particle size distribution of PG-CDs ranges from 1.00 to 4.25 nm with an average particle size of 2.0 nm (Fig. 2b). The crystal structure was revealed by XRD patterns. As depicted in Fig. 2c, PG-CDs showed multiple distinct diffraction peaks different from those of phloroglucinol, indicating that the phloroglucinol has been carbonized completely. The sharp diffraction peaks also implied high crystallinity of PG-CDs [29]. These main diffraction peaks at 17.9°, 24.6°, and 30.5° are attributed to the (100), (002), and (101) diffraction planes of graphitic carbon [30], respectively. Combining TEM and XRD, we infer that graphitized structure exists in PG-CDs.

The composition of PG-CDs was analyzed in detail by FT-IR and XPS. Remarkably, the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2d) of phloroglucinol, O-CDs and PG-CDs exhibited obvious difference, which was an evidence for the generation of PG-CDs. The characteristic absorption peak of stretching vibrations C–N at 1468 cm−1, which indicates the successful doping of N. The broad absorption band at 3440 cm−1 is recognized as the stretching vibration of O–H, showing that –OH group of phloroglucinol was retained and a large amount of –OH on the surface PG-CDs. The obvious peak at 1600 cm−1 is a typical aromatic structure CC bond and the peaks at 3050 and 2784 cm−1 were ascribed to the C–H group on the aromatic ring, suggesting that aromatic ring structures were produced in PG-CDs. Furthermore, the full scan of XPS (Fig. 2e) reveals that PG-CDs is mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, corresponding to 284.5, 400.0 and 531.4 eV, and their contents are 68.41%, 4.93% and 26.66%, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2f) can be fitted to two peaks at 284.6 and 286.2 eV, which can be assigned to C–C/CC (sp2) and C–N/C–O, respectively. The spectrum of N 1s (Fig. S2a) has two peaks at 400.0 and 402.1 eV, corresponding to graphite nitrogen and pyrrole nitrogen, which corroborated the formation of graphitized structure again. The O 1s spectrum peaks at 531.3 and 532.8 eV revealed the dominant CO and C–O groups (Fig. S2b). Combining these results of FT-IR and XPS, we concluded the PG-CDs was composed of aromatic sp2-conjugated domains and a large number of hydroxyl groups on the surface.

The optical properties of PG-CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra in aqueous, firstly. As shown in Fig. 3 a, there are two obvious absorption peaks in the UV–vis absorption spectrum at 286 nm and 472 nm. The absorption peak at 286 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the sp2 domain of the carbon core, and the broad absorption band at 472 nm can be attributed to the functional groups attached to the surface of the PG-CDs [31]. The yellow fluorescence can be observed under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp as PG-CDs were dispersed in water. From the excitation and emission spectra, it can be seen that the optimal excitation and emission wavelength is at 490 and 548 nm, separately. From the spectra in Fig. 3b, as the excitation wavelength varied from 420 to 520 nm, the obtained FL emission peaks are around 548 nm, indicating that PG-CDs have an excitation wavelength-independent characteristic, which can be attributed to the PG-CDs uniform surface state [32].

We found that PG-CDs can be well dispersed in organic solvents such as ethanol, DMF, and acetone, and showed a solvent-dependent behavior. The prepared carbon dots were dispersed in ethanol, methanol, formamide, N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetonitrile, acetone, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and water, the emission peaks are at 525, 528, 535, 536, 537, 539, 539, 542, and 548 nm, respectively (Fig. 3c). When PG-CDs were dispersed in different proportions of ethanol and water, the fluorescence emission peaks at 525 nm red-shifted to 548 nm (Fig. 3d). Some reports conclude that the solvent-dependent of CDs originated from the interactions of hydrogen bond [33], the polar of solvent [34], and surface state [35], but the reasons are still controversial. So we collected the UV–vis absorption spectra, FL lifetimes, and QY to investigate the reason of solvent-dependent behavior.

The absorption spectra are shown in Fig. 3e and f, the absorption peak shifted in the low energy band region around 470 nm, indicating the surface energy levels have been changed. The absorption of this low energy band region corresponds to the surface functional groups of PG-CDs. Therefore, the spectral shift may be caused by the interaction between the surface functional groups and solvents. We know that the polarity of formamide is greater than that of ethanol, the polarity of ethanol is greater than that of acetone, and the PG-CDs in both formamide and acetone have a red shift relative to ethanol. In the solvent of ethanol/water, the shift of emission peak is small when ethanol ≥60%, and the shift of emission peak is obvious in 40%–0% solvent. It can be seen that the shift of the emission spectra did not alternate regularly with the polarity of the solvent. Moreover, the shift of the absorption spectra did not show a regular change with the redshift of the emission spectra. Therefore, the polarity of the solvent is not the main factor causing the change of the emission peak. The solvent-dependent emission may be caused by the interaction between solvent and a large amount of –OH on the surface of PG-CDs. Some precipitation arose from the aqueous of PG-CDs one day later and the solubility in ethanol is also superior to water. As shown in Fig. S3a, the fluorescence intensity of PG-CDs in ethanol was significantly higher than that in water. When the ethanol is less than 40%, the fluorescence intensity was also significantly reduced, which further conclude that the emission of PG-CDs in ethanol solution was mainly influenced by –OH, but not the polarity of the solvent. In addition, the average fluorescence lifetimes of PG-CDs in ethanol and water were 3.77 ns and 2.09 ns, respectively (Fig. S3b), and the fluorescence quantum yields in ethanol and water were 19.9% and 3.3%, respectively (Fig. S3c). The decreasing tendency of the fluorescence lifetimes and quantum yields were correspond to the changing trend of the FL emission peak shift. Based on these experimental results, we assumed that the emission of PG-CDs may be dominated by surface states, and solvent-dependent behavior is due to the effect of surface functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of light and salt concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the stability of PG-CDs for sensing, the effects of light and salt concentrations were explored. As shown in Fig. S4a, under the excitation light of 490 nm for 1 h, the fluorescence intensity of PG-CDs in ethanol has basically no obvious change. It means that PG-CDs has an exceptional resistance to photobleaching and photostability. From Fig. S4b, the FL intensity decreased as the concentration of NaCl increased. So the high salt concentration is not conducive to the stable existence of PG-CDs. Therefore, the detection system with high salt concentrations should be avoided as much as possible.

Interestingly, we found that the fluorescence intensity of PG-CDs decreased with the increase of temperature in DMAC within 15–65 °C (Fig. 4 a). There is a good linear relationship between temperature and fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 (Fig. 4b, F and F0 represent the FL intensity at different temperatures and 15 °C, separately). The fluorescence remained stable after five cycles of heating and cooling at 15 °C and 65 °C (Fig. 4c), implying that PG-CDs have good reversibility and recoverability in response to temperature. Therefore, PG-CDs is a favorable fluorescent probe for temperature sensing. It has broad application prospects in the manufacture of nanometer thermometers.

The mechanism of fluorescence quenching caused by temperature still has been a debate. There are several reports related to the mechanism of temperature-dependent property of CDs, such as nonradiative relaxation [36] and synergistic effect of functional groups and hydrogen bonds [37]. To clarify the mechanism, the fluorescence spectra of PG-CDs dispersed in water and DMF were collected under different temperatures. As shown in Fig. S5a, contrary to in DMAC, the fluorescence intensity increased with the increase of temperature in water. There is no regular linear relationship between temperature and FL intensity. The fluorescence intensity also decreased with the increase of temperature in DMF, and a good linear relationship also can be observed between FL intensity ratio and temperature (Fig. S5b). The difference is that the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of PG-CDs decreased by 26% in DMAC, while the relative fluorescence intensity decreased by 21% in DMF when the temperature increased from 15 °C to 65 °C. According to the slope of the linear curve, the sensitivity of PG-CDs in DMF to temperature is lower than that in DMAC, their sensitivity to temperature is 0.52%°C−1 and 0.42%°C−1, respectively. The different changes may be due to the low solubility of PG-CDs in water. The solubility in water was increased when the temperature was raised, making the PG-CDs more dispersed, the fluorescence was enhanced. However, the PG-CDs can be well dispersed in DMAC and DMF, because of the interaction of –OH on the surface and solvent, PG-CDs shows different sensitivity to temperature. Therefore, we inferred that the temperature-dependent behavior of PG-CDs is ascribed the surface functional groups, which conformed with solvent-dependent behavior.

It was found that the fluorescence intensity of PG-CDs was responsive to the pH of the solution. As shown in Fig. 4d, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with the increase of pH in BR buffer and the emission peak of PG-CDs did not shift obviously. There is a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9982) between the fluorescence intensity of PG-CDs and pH in the range of pH 3.0–7.0 (Fig. 4e). The reversibility of the FL intensity response to pH was explored by adjusting the solution pH between 3.0 and 10.0 repeatedly. From Fig. S6, we can see that the fluorescence intensity always can return to the initial position for five cycles, suggesting that the fluorescent response to pH has good reversibility. Moreover, we tested the possible existence of common interferents for pH determination. As shown in Fig. S7, these common interferents did not influenced in the fluorescence intensity of PG-CDs. It means that PG-CDs has good selectivity and anti-interference for pH sensing. Therefore, PG-CDs is a promising candidate for pH determination, which is expected to be used for intracellular pH sensing and fabrication of nano pH meters.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were collected to investigate the mechanism for fluorescent response to pH. As shown in Fig. 4f, the intensities of the two absorption bands near 285 nm and 470 nm gradually decreased when the pH increased from 2.0 to 5.0. The absorption peak in the lower energy regions occurred blue-shifted at pH from 5.0 to 7.0. Some new absorption peaks appeared obviously as the pH varied from 7.0 to 11.0. The difference of absorption spectra may be ascribed to the different surface states [35]. It is probably because the functional groups were changed in different pH, the FL was quenched. The result was in accordance with the solvent-dependent and temperature-dependent behaviors.

In order to make the proton pump inhibitors have a better response, we optimized the pH and reaction time of the system in detail. For EPZ and OPZ, the maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F0–F)/F0) was obtained at pH 3.5 and 4.0 (Fig. S8a and Fig. S8b), so the optimum pH for EPZ and OPZ sensing is 3.5 and 4.0 respectively. Though the quenching efficiency increased as pH increased from 2.5 to 5.5 for RPZ (Fig. S8c), the fluorescence of PG-CDs is weaker at higher pH, so pH 4.0 was selected for RPZ determination. From Figs. S8d–f, as the reaction time became longer, the fluorescence quenching efficiency first increased, and the reactions of the three PPI remained stable after 1 h. Therefore, the reactions were incubated for 1 h before detection.

Under the selected assay conditions, the fluorescence in the presence of different concentrations of PPI was monitored. As displayed in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence was quenched gradually with the increasing concentration of EPZ, OPZ, and RPZ. Good linear relationships were obtained between PPI concentrations and logarithm of fluorescence intensity ratio (Ln (F0/F), F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity with and without PPI), which can monitor EPZ, OPZ, and RPZ in 2.0–80.0, 2.0–75.0, and 10.0–200.0 μM, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) for EPZ, OPZ, and RPZ are calculated to be 0.29, 0.55, and 2.99 μM (signal-to-noise ratio of 3), respectively. The results were also listed in Table S1. A comparison with reported methods in Table S2. shows our essay possessed favorable linear ranges and lower LOD, which exhibited promising analytical ability.

Then, the mechanism for PPI sensing was studied with EPZ as the representative. The common UV–vis absorption spectra were investigated at first, including the EPZ, PG-CDs, the mixtures of PG-CDs and EPZ (Fig. 6 a). It was found that the measured and theoretical absorption spectra of the mixture of EPZ and PG-CDS did not overlap completely. The absorption peak of PG-CDs at 470 nm was red-shifted to 480 nm after adding EPZ and there are also differences in the absorption intensity. It means that new association compounds were formed in the system and static quenching was generated. We compared the UV–Vis absorption spectra of EPZ at pH 3.5 and pH 7.0. As shown in Fig. S9, there are obvious differences in the absorption spectra at different pH. It means that EPZ exists in different forms. So pH affect the existing forms of EPZ, and that acidic conditions may promoted the generation of static quenching process. The fluorescence lifetimes were conducted subsequently in the presence and absence of EPZ to know exactly mechanism of FL quenching. As shown in Fig. 6b, the average fluorescence lifetime of PG-CDs was 2.06 ns without EPZ, and decreased to 0.99 ns in the presence of EPZ. The fluorescence lifetime decreased obviously, which indicates dynamic quenching or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) may exist. However, there is no obvious overlap between emission spectrum and absorption spectrum in Fig. S10. FRET was excluded and dynamic quenching may occur. To verify this conjecture, the Stern-Volmer curve was drawn. As depicted in Fig. S11, the obtained Stern-Volmer curve in the detection system is not a straight line, but an upward curve curved to the Y-axis, indicating that the quenching mechanism is a combination of dynamic and static quenching. Moreover, we tested and analyzed the Stern-Volmer curves at 288 K and 308 K. As displayed in Fig. 6c, the Stern-Volmer curves at two different temperatures were curved upwards. More importantly, as the temperature enhanced, F0/F increased faster, indicating that dynamic quenching plays a major role in the system. As the temperature increasing, the stability of the associated complex decreased and the quenching rate constants declined for static quenching, while the ability of collision enhanced and the quenching rate constant increased for dynamic quenching. Thus, dynamic quenching dominated in the system. At present, there are few reports on the sensing of drugs based on synergistic effect of dynamic and static quenching using carbon dots as fluorescent probes. This finding not only expanded the application of CDs in drug analysis, but also provided a new idea for drug analysis and biochemical sensing.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of PPI sensing, a series of interfering substances (200 μM) were tested for their effect on the fluorescence of PG-CDs. From Fig. 6d, we can see that PG-CDs did not response to these interferents obviously except for EPZ, common interferents basically did not cause significant fluorescence quenching. This illustrated that PG-CDs had good selectivity for the detection of proton pump inhibitors.

The practicability of PG-CDs-based probe for PPI sensing was assessed in serum sample. As shown in Table 1 , EPZ was not detected in the serum of healthy volunteer. The prepared real samples were spiked into solutions with known concentrations of EPZ for recovery tests. The results showed that the recovery rate of EPZ in human serum was between 90.08 and 104.7%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was between 1.1 and 2.3%. The satisfactory recoveries indicate the approach is reliable for PPI sensing.

In summary, a novel solvent-dependent fluorescent CDs were prepared by solvothermal method from phloroglucinol. The as-prepared PG-CDs has been implemented in the measurement of temperature and pH with good linear relationships and reversibility. These behaviors of solvent-dependent, temperature-dependent, and pH-dependent were ascribed to the effects of functional groups on the surface. Furthermore, PG-CDs has been utilized to the sensing of PPI via the mechanism of synergy of dynamic and static quenching for the first time. The platform for sensing of PPI has high sensitivity and satisfactory recoveries in human serum. Our research can be applied as a promising candidate for multifunctional sensing of temperature, pH, and PPI. The multifunctional sensor platform further improves efficiency of analytical detection and expands the application field of CDs.

Piao Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jingdong Peng: Conceptualization, Resources, Methodology, Supervision, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zilong Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Xiang Wang: Formal analysis, Investigation. Xiaolan Zhu: Conceptualization, Investigation. Kun Fan: Investigation. Pan Luo: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors. The authors special thanks the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the tests of XPS and TEM.
